title: Weekly Reflections
text:
AI and originality
This week, I learned how AI can enhance learning without replacing original thought. AI is beneficial for me when used as a support tool for understanding concepts, while originality comes from how ideas are interpreted and communicated by me. I use AI to help me study! For example,it is beneficial for me to break down difficult concepts so that I can understand them in more simple terms and real-world scenario examples.

title: Weekly Reflections
section: What is AGI? How does AGI affect scientific research?
text:
What is AGI? How does AGI affect scientific research?
I learned that Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) refers to systems capable of performing human level tasks. AGI could help enhance scientific research through faster data analysis, but it also causes ethical concerns in regards to responsible use.

text:
How to start using AI for your project?
I believe I can use AI in my project by helping me turn our STATA data zip file to help us use it in R studio. AI can help us let the data be read in R studio.

text:

I am using CHATGPT and Google Gemini for my project. I am new to using R, so I will ask it to help me if I run into any errors uploading the STATA zip file to R studio.

title: Weekly Reflections
text: WEEK 3 REFLECTION

title: Weekly Reflections
section: How do humans find knowledge? How is knowledge different from information?
text:
How do humans find knowledge? How is knowledge different from information?
People find knowledge by noticing patterns in information and linking them to what they already understand. Information is data that has been organized, but knowledge is when you understand the reasons behind things and how they connect. When we mine for knowledge, we look for useful patterns and remove what is not important.

title: Weekly Reflections
section: List of AI discrepancies/failures. Any pattern?
text:
List of AI discrepancies/failures. Any pattern?
AI systems can make errors such as generating fabricated information, perpetuating biases, or identifying patterns that lack significance. These issues arise when AI models detect patterns without understanding their meaning. The primary challenge is that AI may mistake genuine knowledge for random or biased data.

title: Weekly Reflections
text: WEEK 4 REFLECTION

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Start your first ML pipeline.
A basic machine learning pipeline starts with collecting data, cleaning it, splitting it into training and test sets, training a model, and checking its results. Each step turns raw data into information the model can use. This is similar to knowledge mining because it finds patterns in data one step at a time.

title: Weekly Reflections
section: What did error analysis reveal that accuracy alone did not? How does overfitting relate to knowledge vs. noise?
text:
What did error analysis reveal that accuracy alone did not? How does overfitting relate to knowledge vs. noise?
Error analysis showed where the model made mistakes, which accuracy alone did not reveal. Accuracy is just a summary, but error analysis explains why errors happen. Overfitting is when a model learns random noise instead of the main patterns that show real knowledge.

title: Weekly Reflections
text: WEEK 5 REFLECTION

title: Weekly Reflections
section: Prediction vs. explanation: when does it matter why something happens, and when is it enough to know what will happen? Give a policy or research example.
text:
Prediction vs. explanation: when does it matter why something happens, and when is it enough to know what will happen? Give a policy or research example.
Prediction helps us see what might happen, like which neighborhoods could face high eviction rates next year. Explanation is needed when we want to fix the problem. We need to know why evictions happen to make good policies. For example, a model might predict eviction based on rent debt, but we also need to check if job loss, higher rents, or weak tenant protections are causing it. If we only look at predictions, we might act too late or choose solutions that do not work.

title: Weekly Reflections
section: Draw a simple causal diagram for a relationship in your research area. What confounders exist? How would you distinguish a causal claim from a predictive association?
text:
Draw a simple causal diagram for a relationship in your research area. What confounders exist? How would you distinguish a causal claim from a predictive association?
A simple causal diagram is: education level affects income. Confounders are things like family background, neighborhood, school quality, and work experience. These can change both education and income. A causal claim says education changes income. A predictive association just means the two are linked. To tell the difference, I would use experiments or control for confounders with statistics.

title: Weekly Reflections
text: WEEK 6 REFLECTION

title: Weekly Reflections
section: How can text mining reveal patterns that close reading alone cannot? What are its blind spots?
text:
How can text mining reveal patterns that close reading alone cannot? What are its blind spots?
Text mining lets us look at lots of documents quickly. It finds common words, themes, and trends. Close reading gives more detail but is too slow for big collections. Text mining can miss sarcasm, tone, or hidden meaning. It can also make common words seem more important than they are.

title: Weekly Reflections
section: How NLP and LLM can assist in your research and provide data/assistance for your project? Give specific examples.
text:
How NLP and LLM can assist in your research and provide data/assistance for your project? Give specific examples.
Natural language processing helps clean text, find key terms, and spot common topics. It can also compare documents over time. Large language models can summarize long reports, suggest coding ideas, and explain patterns in simple words. For example, in a job-posting project, NLP can count how often skills like data analysis or policy appear. An LLM can group similar job descriptions. In a defense report project, these tools can track changes in words about strategy, risk, or threats.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Name limitations and suggest solutions.
One problem is bias in the data, which can lead to unfair results. Another is hallucination, where the model gives wrong information but sounds sure. Loss of context is also an issue, especially in short summaries. To fix these, we need to check outputs by hand, use trusted sources, report uncertainty, and keep humans involved.

title: Weekly Reflections
section: Write an AI for research guide (you can use bullet points) incorporating ethical, technical and academic concerns that could lead to more problems than solutions (e.g. Hinton).
text:
Write an AI for research guide (you can use bullet points) incorporating ethical, technical and academic concerns that could lead to more problems than solutions (e.g. Hinton).

Use AI to support research, not replace human judgment.
Always verify facts, quotes, and citations with original sources.
Do not paste private, sensitive, or protected data into AI tools.
Report when AI was used and explain how it was used.
Watch for bias, hallucinations, and weak reasoning before trusting results.

title: Weekly Reflections
text: WEEK 7 REFLECTION

title: Weekly Reflections
section: What can LLMs do well, and where do they fail? Evaluate one specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, reasoning error) you have encountered.
text:
What can LLMs do well, and where do they fail? Evaluate one specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, reasoning error) you have encountered.
Large language models are good at summarizing, giving ideas, rewriting text, and explaining hard topics in simple words. They are not as good when they give wrong information but sound confident. I have seen hallucination, where the model makes up sources or details that are not in the data. This is risky in research because false information can look true if it is written clearly.

title: Weekly Reflections
section: How might generative AI reshape your discipline in the next five years? What research tasks will change most, and what should remain human-driven?
text:
How might generative AI reshape your discipline in the next five years? What research tasks will change most, and what should remain human-driven?
Generative AI will likely speed up research by helping with coding, summarizing, drafting, and text analysis. Literature reviews, first drafts, and sorting documents will change the most. But humans should still choose research questions, pick theories, handle ethics, and explain results. Researchers also need to check for accuracy, fairness, and real-world effects.

title: Weekly Reflections
text: WEEK 8 REFLECTION

title: Weekly Reflections
section: What can LLMs do well, and where do they fail? Evaluate one specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, reasoning error) you have encountered.
text:
What can LLMs do well, and where do they fail? Evaluate one specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, reasoning error) you have encountered.
Large language models are good at organizing information, giving examples, and helping people start tasks quickly. They do not work as well when the data or prompts cause unfair or unbalanced answers. I have seen bias, where some groups or views are not shown fairly. This matters because biased results can affect research, coding, and policy in bad ways.

title: Weekly Reflections
section: How might generative AI reshape your discipline in the next five years? What research tasks will change most, and what should remain human-driven?
text:
How might generative AI reshape your discipline in the next five years? What research tasks will change most, and what should remain human-driven?
In the next five years, generative AI will likely be a common tool for text analysis, cleaning data, and first drafts. It will make repetitive tasks faster and help researchers work with bigger datasets. But humans should still handle causal reasoning, ethics, and final decisions. Research should stay human-led where judgment, responsibility, and trust matter most.

Studying liability claims and emerging technology risks, including how artificial intelligence is influencing legal processes and perceptions of harm, clarified my interest in claims evaluation and loss analysis. I learned that careful documentation, policy interpretation, and analytical judgment directly shape outcomes for both insurers and insureds.



CPCU Award


These experiences strengthened my critical thinking, attention to detail, and ethical decision-making, confirming that claims, where legal reasoning, analytics, and risk assessment come together, is where I can make the greatest impact.

title: Assignment 2 – Prompt Exercise
text: This assignment shows how different AI models respond to the same research task. The goal is to design prompts, evaluate model outputs, refine instructions, and understand how prompt structure impacts response quality.

title: Assignment 2 – Prompt Exercise
text: This assignment shows how different AI models respond to the same research task. The goal is to design prompts, evaluate model outputs, refine instructions, and understand how prompt structure impacts response quality.

title: Assignment 2 – Prompt Exercise
text:
Design Prompt
For the first test, I submitted the following prompt to multiple AI models:
Write a 1,500 word literature review that reads like an analytical discussion rather than a textbook summary. Start by explaining the purpose and scope of the review. Include a methodology section describing how ideas, themes, and findings are compared across studies. Add key insights by identifying patterns, similarities, and differences. Highlight important trends, challenges, and meaningful research gaps. Finish by suggesting one realistic and testable hypothesis that logically follows from the discussion. Maintain a formal but clear writing style, prioritizing explanation and reasoning over definitions or lists.
The prompt was submitted to ChatGPT, Copilot, and Grok 3.

title: Assignment 2 – Prompt Exercise
text:
Model Response Analysis
Each model response was evaluated using the following criteria:
Structure
Did it include a methodology section and follow a systematic review format?
Synthesis
Were key findings from data mining and machine learning applications well-summarized?
Trends and Gaps
Did it identify meaningful trends and research gaps?
Hypothesis Quality
Was the proposed hypothesis testable and relevant?
References
Are the citations accurate (check using Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar)

title: Assignment 2 – Prompt Exercise
text:
Analyze Model Responses
Structure
Regarding the structure, ChatGPT did a great job because it included a specific section explaining its methodology and followed a professional format. Copilot was also very organized and used clear headings that made the report easy to navigate. Grok 3 included a methodology paragraph as well, but the overall response felt more like one long essay rather than a structured review with clear sections.
Synthesis
When it comes to summarizing data mining and machine learning, ChatGPT was the only model that successfully stayed on topic. It explained how researchers are trying to balance making models accurate while also making them easy for humans to understand. Copilot completely missed the mark here because it wrote about technology in classrooms instead of data mining. Grok 3 focused almost entirely on how AI affects jobs and the economy, which was interesting but didn’t cover enough of the technical machine learning details.
Trends and Gaps
All three models tried to identify trends, but ChatGPT was the most relevant to the prompt by pointing out that researchers are moving away from simple predictions and looking for deeper causes. Copilot identified gaps in how technology affects different students, which was good but for the wrong subject. Grok 3 identified a very modern trend regarding “agentic AI” and noted that we still don’t have enough long-term data on how these systems behave.
Hypothesis Quality
The hypotheses provided were a bit of a mixed bag. ChatGPT created a strong, testable guess about whether adaptive models work better than static ones over a long period of time. Copilot also wrote a clear hypothesis about student learning, but it wasn’t relevant to the actual assignment topic of data mining. Grok 3 failed this part entirely because the response cut off at the end, so it never actually provided a hypothesis to test.
References
For the references and citations, all of the models struggled. None of them provided a list of specific papers, authors, or links that could be double-checked on Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar. They all spoke about “the literature” in a general way without giving credit to specific researchers, which makes it hard to verify if the information is coming from a real study.
Strengths and Weaknesses
In summary, ChatGPT’s main strength was following the instructions perfectly and staying on the right topic, but its weakness was the lack of real citations. Copilot’s strength was its very clean and easy-to-read structure, but its major weakness was hallucinating the wrong topic for the review. Grok 3’s strength was providing very deep and unique thoughts about current AI trends, but its biggest weakness was being incomplete and cutting off before finishing the task.

title: Assignment 2 – Prompt Exercise
text:
Refined Prompts
After reviewing weaknesses, I revised the prompts.
Refined Prompt for ChatGPT
The biggest problem with ChatGPT’s first answer was that it did not name any real scientists or papers. It spoke in a very general way, which makes it hard to prove the information is true. This new prompt tells ChatGPT that it must include specific names and dates for its sources. It also asks for a bibliography at the end so we can check the work using Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar to make sure the citations are accurate.
New Prompt for ChatGPT:
Write a 1,500-word literature review about data mining and machine learning. You must include real in-text citations from academic studies, such as (Author, Year), to support your points. Include a methodology section that explains how you compared different studies. At the very end, provide a full list of references and one realistic, testable hypothesis that researchers could use for a new study.
Refined Prompt for Microsoft Copilot
Microsoft Copilot failed the first time because it wrote about the wrong topic, focusing on education instead of computer science. To fix this, the new prompt uses very strict instructions to stay on the subject of data mining and machine learning. Since Copilot is good at making things look neat, the prompt asks for a clear structure with specific sections for trends and gaps. This will help the model stay focused on the technical requirements of the assignment.
New Prompt for Copilot:
Write an analytical literature review of exactly 1,500 words about the technical applications of Data Mining and Machine Learning. Do not write about “human learning” or “education”; you must focus only on computer science topics like algorithm performance and data patterns. Organize the review with clear sections for methodology, key findings, and research gaps. End the discussion with a formal, testable hypothesis.
Refined Prompt for Grok 3
Grok 3’s first response was smart, but it was too short and it cut off before it could finish the job. It also focused too much on jobs and money instead of the actual technology. The new prompt tells Grok 3 to act like a data scientist and write a much longer review. It also tells the model to balance its time better so that it does not stop talking before it gets to the hypothesis at the very end.
New Prompt for Grok 3:
Imagine you are a data scientist writing a 2,000-word systematic literature review on data mining and machine learning in fields like healthcare and finance. You must provide a clear methodology and focus on the technical side of how models work, such as their accuracy and how they handle complex data. Ensure you manage the length of your response so it is complete. You must finish with a bold and testable hypothesis and a final conclusion.

title: Assignment 2 – Prompt Exercise
text:
Final Prompt
After testing the new prompts, the results were much better because each AI finally followed the specific rules it missed the first time. ChatGPT became more trustworthy by adding real names and dates of scientists to support its ideas, while Copilot finally stopped talking about the wrong topic and stayed focused only on computer science. Grok 3 also did a better job by finishing its entire report and providing a smart “science guess” or hypothesis at the end instead of cutting off early. This shows that when you give an AI very clear instructions and fix its past mistakes, it can write a much more professional and complete paper for your project.
After collecting revised outputs, I used this synthesis prompt:
I am giving you three different drafts for a literature review on Data Mining and Machine Learning. Please combine them into one final 2,000-word systematic review. Use the real-world citations and the methodology from the first draft, the clear headings and organization from the second draft, and the modern technical insights from the third draft. Make sure the final document flows smoothly, maintains a formal tone, and ends with the most realistic and testable hypothesis. [I insert the three model outputs here.]

title: Assignment 2 – Prompt Exercise
text:
Reflection
How did the models respond differently?
Each model handled the systematic review in a different way. ChatGPT focused on the technical history and professional structure of data mining, making sure to connect old methods with new ones. Microsoft Copilot focused more on the layout and organization of the report, though it struggled to stay on the correct topic at the beginning. Grok 3 focused on the newest trends and how AI affects the real world right now, but it had a hard time finishing long responses and often stopped before the end.
Which refinements worked best?
The prompt refinements that worked best were the ones that added very specific requirements. For ChatGPT, telling it to include real-world citations with authors and dates made the review much more accurate. For Copilot, the most helpful change was using bold instructions to stay strictly on the topic of computer science so it wouldn’t talk about other subjects. For Grok 3, the best fix was asking it to be more concise and watch its length, which helped it complete the final sections and the hypothesis.
What did this exercise demonstrate?
I learned that using AI for academic reviews requires a lot of back-and-forth work to get a good result. You cannot just use the first answer the AI gives you; you have to check for missing parts and fix the instructions to get better details. I also learned that it is best to use multiple models because one might be better at organizing while another is better at finding modern trends. The most important lesson is that a human must guide the AI and verify all the facts to make sure the final paper is correct and complete.
AI Disclaimer: I used ChatGPT as a tutor to clarify instructions, troubleshoot errors, and better understand mistakes encountered during the process. It also assisted with wording and formatting; however, the final submission reflects my own work and judgment.

title: Assignment 3: ML Exercise
text:
Multiple Regression Exercise
This assignment applies ideas from ISLR Chapter 3 on linear regression. Multiple regression is used when one dependent variable is modeled using more than one predictor at the same time. In this exercise, the TEDS2016 dataset is used to run a multiple regression model and to create regression plots showing the relationship between the dependent variable and Age, Education, and Income.

title: Assignment 3: ML Exercise
text:
2. Review ISLR Chapters 3
ISLR Chapter 3 provides the background for this assignment by explaining simple linear regression and multiple linear regression. The chapter shows that regression is used to model the relationship between a response variable and one or more predictors, and that in a multiple regression model each coefficient represents the effect of one predictor while holding the other predictors fixed.

title: Assignment 3: ML Exercise
text:
3. Load the TEDS2016 dataset
The next step is to load the TEDS2016 dataset into R using the exact code provided in the assignment. This is necessary because the dataset must be available in the R environment before the regression model and regression plots can be created.

library(haven)

TEDS_2016 <- read_stata("https://github.com/datageneration/home/blob/master/DataProgramming/data/TEDS_2016.dta?raw=true")

title: Assignment 3: ML Exercise
text:
4. Run the multiple regression model
After loading the dataset, the next step is to run a multiple regression model. Multiple regression is appropriate when the response variable is modeled using several predictors simultaneously. In this case, PartyID is used as the dependent variable, while Age, Edu, and income are used as predictors. This model estimates how each predictor is associated with the dependent variable while the other predictors are held constant.

lm.fit <- lm(PartyID ~ Age + Edu + income, data = TEDS_2016)
summary(lm.fit)


Call:
lm(formula = PartyID ~ Age + Edu + income, data = TEDS_2016)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-5.038 -2.963 -2.121  4.135  6.622 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  7.00058    0.42994  16.283  < 2e-16 ***
Age         -0.28172    0.07176  -3.926 9.00e-05 ***
Edu         -0.29039    0.06906  -4.205 2.75e-05 ***
income      -0.10909    0.03301  -3.304 0.000972 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 3.511 on 1686 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.02327,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.02153 
F-statistic: 13.39 on 3 and 1686 DF,  p-value: 1.233e-08


The regression summary provides the estimated coefficients, standard errors, t-values, p-values, and overall model fit statistics. The coefficients show the direction of the relationship for each predictor, the p-values show whether those relationships appear statistically significant, and the residual standard error and multiple R-squared describe how well the model fits the data overall.

title: Assignment 3: ML Exercise
text:
5. Create the regplot function
The assignment then asks for a function called regplot to draw a regression fit plot. This function combines lm(), plot(), and abline() to create a scatterplot and add the fitted regression line in red. Writing the function once makes it possible to reuse the same plotting method for each predictor.

regplot=function(x,y){
 fit=lm(y~x)
 plot(x,y)
 abline(fit,col="red")
}

title: Assignment 3: ML Exercise
text:
5a. Run a regplot using Age
The first regression plot uses Age as the explanatory variable and the dependent variable as the response. This makes it possible to visually examine whether the dependent variable tends to increase or decrease as Age changes.

regplot(TEDS_2016$Age, TEDS_2016$PartyID)

title: Assignment 3: ML Exercise
text:
6. What is the problem? Why?
The main problem is that the dependent variable is not a continuous outcome. Instead, it consists of discrete categories, so the points in the regression plots appear in stacked horizontal levels rather than along a continuous scale. Because ordinary linear regression is designed for a quantitative continuous response variable, the fitted regression line is not the most appropriate way to model or predict this type of outcome.

title: Assignment 3: ML Exercise
section: 7. What can be done to improve prediction of the dependent variable?
text:
7. What can be done to improve prediction of the dependent variable?
Prediction can be improved by using a model that is designed for a categorical dependent variable rather than ordinary linear regression. A better choice would be multinomial logistic regression if the dependent variable has several categories. Another possible improvement would be to recode the dependent variable into fewer meaningful groups before modeling.

title: Cyber Insurance Research
text:
This project for my senior capstone analyzes a CSV file of breach descriptions as a text corpus. The goal is to identify the most common claim language, incident types, PHI categories, mitigation actions, and broader topic patterns mentioned across the records.
The workflow follows a text-mining structure that moves from spreadsheet text to a corpus, then to tokens, and then to a document-feature matrix for visual analysis. This makes it possible to compare repeated language patterns across breach descriptions while preserving the full dataset.

title: Cyber Insurance Research
text:
17.1 Intertopic Map Overview
This section is separate from the earlier graphs because it uses topic modeling rather than simple word-frequency analysis. The goal is to uncover broader themes in the breach descriptions and visualize how similar or different those themes are from one another.
The labels shown on the map are researcher-assigned summaries based on each topic’s most defining words. This means the model identifies the word patterns first, and the final topic labels are then interpreted in plain language.

title: Cyber Insurance Research
section: 17.2 Step 1: Check Required Packages and Objects

title: Cyber Insurance Research
section: 17.3 Step 2: Trim the Document-Feature Matrix for Topic Modeling
text:
17.3 Step 2: Trim the Document-Feature Matrix for Topic Modeling
This step creates a version of the document-feature matrix specifically for topic modeling. Very rare terms and overly common terms are trimmed so that the model focuses on words that are more useful for identifying themes.

dfmat_topic <- quanteda::dfm_trim(
  dfmat,
  min_termfreq = 5,
  min_docfreq = 2,
  max_docfreq = floor(quanteda::ndoc(dfmat) * 0.85),
  docfreq_type = "count"
)

if (quanteda::nfeat(dfmat_topic) < 10) {
  stop("Too few terms remain after trimming for topic modeling. Lower min_termfreq or min_docfreq and try again.")
}

cat("Topic-modeling DFM:", quanteda::ndoc(dfmat_topic), "documents and", quanteda::nfeat(dfmat_topic), "features\n")

Topic-modeling DFM: 5008 documents and 1987 features

title: Cyber Insurance Research
section: 17.4 Step 3: Convert the Matrix and Fit the LDA Model
text:
17.4 Step 3: Convert the Matrix and Fit the LDA Model
This step converts the quanteda matrix into the format required by topicmodels, then fits a Latent Dirichlet Allocation model. The model is set to estimate five topics.

dtm_topic <- quanteda::convert(dfmat_topic, to = "topicmodels")

set.seed(1234)
k <- 5

lda_fit <- topicmodels::LDA(
  dtm_topic,
  k = k,
  control = list(seed = 1234)
)

lda_fit

A LDA_VEM topic model with 5 topics.

title: Cyber Insurance Research
section: 17.5 Step 4: Extract Topic-Term and Document-Topic Probabilities
text:
17.5 Step 4: Extract Topic-Term and Document-Topic Probabilities
This step pulls out the two main outputs of the topic model:

phi: the probability of each term within each topic
theta: the probability of each topic within each document

These are the core parts for labeling topics and building the intertopic map.

post <- topicmodels::posterior(lda_fit)

phi <- post$terms
theta <- post$topics

dim(phi)

[1]    5 1987

dim(theta)

[1] 5007    5

section: 17.6 Step 5: Get the Top Words for Each Topic
text:
17.6 Step 5: Get the Top Words for Each Topic
This step identifies the most important words within each topic. These top words are later used to create readable topic labels and interpretations.

top_terms_list <- apply(phi, 1, function(x) {
  names(sort(x, decreasing = TRUE))[1:8]
})

top_terms_df <- tibble::tibble(
  topic_id = 1:k,
  top_terms = vapply(1:k, function(i) paste(top_terms_list[, i], collapse = ", "), character(1))
)

knitr::kable(
  top_terms_df,

)

text:
17.10 Step 9: Calculate Dominant Topic Share
This step calculates the share of documents assigned most strongly to each topic.
This makes topic prevalence easier to interpret than simply averaging topic probabilities, which can look artificially even across topics.

dominant_topic <- apply(theta, 1, which.max)

topic_prevalence <- round(
  100 * prop.table(table(factor(dominant_topic, levels = 1:k))),
  1
)

topic_prevalence <- as.numeric(topic_prevalence)

prevalence_df <- tibble::tibble(
  topic_id = 1:k,
  prevalence_percent = topic_prevalence
)

text:
17.11 Step 10: Build the Final Topic Map Dataset
This step combines the topic labels, meanings, coordinates, top terms, and prevalence values into one final dataset for plotting.

pink_palette <- c("#d63384", "#e83e8c", "#f06595", "#ff85a1", "#ff99c8")

topic_map <- tibble::tibble(
  topic_id = 1:k,
  label = topic_labels,
  x = coords[, 1],
  y = coords[, 2],
  prevalence = as.numeric(topic_prevalence),
  top_terms = vapply(seq_len(k), function(i) paste(top_terms_list[, i], collapse = ", "), character(1)),
  meaning = topic_meanings,
  color = pink_palette[seq_len(k)]
)

knitr::kable(
  topic_map %>%
    dplyr::select(topic_id, label, prevalence, top_terms, meaning),
  caption = "Final topic map dataset used in the interactive plot"

section: 17.12 Step 11: Create the Interactive Intertopic Map
text:
17.12 Step 11: Create the Interactive Intertopic Map
This is the final visualization step. Each bubble is a topic, the size reflects prevalence, the placement reflects similarity to other topics, and the hover text shows the topic meaning and top defining words.

bubble_sizes <- scales::rescale(as.numeric(topic_map$prevalence), to = c(35, 90))

plotly::plot_ly(
  data = topic_map,
  x = ~x,
  y = ~y,
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers+text",
  text = ~label,
  textposition = "top center",
  hovertemplate = ~paste0(
    "<b>", label, "</b><br>",
    "Dominant-topic share: ", prevalence, "%<br>",
    "Top terms: ", top_terms, "<br>",
    "Meaning: ", meaning,
    "<extra></extra>"
  ),
  marker = list(
    size = bubble_sizes,
    color = topic_map$color,
    opacity = 0.80,
    line = list(color = "#8a0f4d", width = 1.5)
  )
) %>%
  plotly::layout(
    title = list(text = "Interactive Intertopic Distance Map"),
    xaxis = list(title = "Dimension 1"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Dimension 2")
  )

title: Cyber Insurance Research
text:
17.13 What the Output Means
The intertopic map should be read as a visual summary of how the latent topics relate to one another. If two bubbles are close together, that means those topics use similar vocabulary and are thematically related. If two bubbles are farther apart, that means their word distributions are more distinct.
Bubble size reflects dominant-topic share. A larger bubble means that topic is the main theme in more documents, while a smaller bubble means it is dominant in fewer documents.
The words shown in the hover text are the highest-probability terms for that topic.
These are the terms that most strongly define the topic according to the model.
The topic names are interpretive labels assigned after inspecting those top words.
In other words, the model discovers the word patterns first, and the final topic name is the human-readable explanation of what that pattern appears to represent.

title: Cyber Insurance Research
text:
17.14 Topic Modeling Notes
This section differs from the earlier graphs because it does not simply count words or categories. Instead, it uses co-occurrence patterns across documents to estimate latent topics, which can reveal broader thematic structure in the breach narratives.
